## {background-color="#533860"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.8em; font-weight: bold;"}
🧬 Postdoc: Population Genetics
:::
::: {style="color: #FFE42A; font-size: 1.1em;"}
How we use statistics to learn about human history
:::

## What is genetic data? {#pop-genetics}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- DNA packaged into **chromosomes**
- Humans: 23 pairs
- Each position in the genome can vary between individuals
- Variation = signal about history

![](images/chromosomes.png)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](images/03GENOME3-jumbo.jpg)
:::
::::

## Big picture questions {#pop-gen-questions}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Question 1:** What was the past size and structure of populations?

![](images/outofafrica.png)
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
**Question 2:** Where in the genome is natural selection occurring?

- Which genes gave us an advantage?
- Which variants spread — and why?
:::
:::
::::

## The modeling framework {#pop-gen-inference}

**Goal:** turn data into answers about history

::: {.fragment}
$$\text{Assumptions} + \text{Data} \xrightarrow{\text{model}} \text{Likelihood (Probability)}$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
- **Assumptions:** population size history, mutation rate, selection strength
- **Data:** sequencing data from real individuals
- **Output:** probability of seeing that data under our assumptions
:::

::: {.fragment}
> Vary the assumptions → find the ones that make the data most probable
:::

## Let's build a model! {#wf-intro}

![](images/builder_mario_and_builder_luigi.png){.r-stretch fig-align="center"}

## Wright-Fisher Model: Setup {#wf-setup}

::: {.incremental}
- Focus on **one position** in the genome
- Two versions ("alleles") at that site:
  - [**Mario**]{style="color:red;"} and [**Luigi**]{style="color:green;"}
- Population size **N** (fixed each generation)
- Time moves in **discrete generations**
- Each offspring **randomly picks a parent** from the previous generation
- Track $p_k$ = proportion of [Luigis]{style="color:green;"} in generation $k$
:::

## Wright-Fisher Model: Visual {#wf-visual}

![](images/WFImageLast.jpg){.r-stretch fig-align="center"}

## Computers can roll dice! {#wf-simulation}

**Key insight:** random sampling = rolling dice

::: {.fragment}
- N = 1,000 individuals?
- Each generation: sample 1,000 parents (with replacement)
- Fraction of [Luigis]{style="color:green;"} drifts up and down randomly
:::

::: {.fragment}
```r
# Simulate one generation
sample(c("Mario", "Luigi"), size = 1000, replace = TRUE,
       prob = c(1 - p, p))
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
- No calculus needed — just repeat millions of times
- This is called **genetic drift**
:::

## Simulations: A few paths {#wf-few-paths}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
- Start: 50% [Luigi]{style="color:green;"}, 50% [Mario]{style="color:red;"}
- Each line = one simulated population
- [Luigi proportion]{style="color:green;"} wanders randomly over generations
- Different random histories → different outcomes
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.fragment}
- Small population → faster drift
- Large population → slower drift
- Eventually... one allele wins
:::
:::
::::

## Simulations: Many paths → fixation {#wf-many-paths}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
With 1,000 simulated populations:

::: {.incremental}
- Paths **spread out** over time
- Eventually all reach 0% or 100%
- Called **fixation** — one allele takes over
- All [Mario]{style="color:red;"} or all [Luigi]{style="color:green;"}
:::
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
**Adding mutation prevents fixation:**

- Occasionally [Mario]{style="color:red;"} mutates → [Luigi]{style="color:green;"} (and vice versa)
- Mutation rate $\mu \approx 10^{-4}$ per generation
- Keeps both alleles in the population
- Distribution of proportions reaches a **steady state**
:::
:::
::::

## From model to inference {#wf-inference}

Back to the inference triangle:

::: {.fragment}
$$\text{Assumptions} + \text{Data} \xrightarrow{\text{WF model}} \text{Likelihood}$$
:::

::: {.incremental}
- Run the WF model under many different population histories
- Each history gives a different likelihood for the observed data
- **Maximum likelihood:** pick the history with the highest probability
- This is how we estimate things like: *when did humans leave Africa?*
:::

## Activity: Out of Africa {#out-of-africa}

![](images/outofafrica.png){.r-stretch fig-align="center"}

## Toy model: population size history {#toy-model}

A simple three-phase model:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
::: {.incremental}
1. **Large ancestral population** — stable for many generations
2. **Bottleneck** — small group migrates out of Africa
   - Population size drops sharply
3. **Exponential growth** — population expands in new environment
:::
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
The shape looks like:

- Flat → sharp dip → rapid rise

Parameters to estimate:

- When did the bottleneck happen?
- How small did it get?
:::
:::
::::

## Your job: estimate the bottleneck {#activity}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
- Open the Shiny app: [https://YOUR-APP-URL](https://YOUR-APP-URL)
- Adjust sliders to match the observed genetic data
- **Hints:**
  1. Population before bottleneck: **1,000**
  2. Total generations: **1,000**
  3. Growth begins at generation **900**
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.fragment}
**What you're estimating:**

- Time of bottleneck (in generations)
- Bottleneck population size

This is real statistical inference — same idea researchers use on actual human genomic data!
:::
:::
::::

## Undergraduate research highlight {#anna-bauer}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
![](images/anna.jpg)
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
**Anna Bauer**

- Double major: Math & Biology
- Junior
- Goal: PhD in Bioinformatics

**Project:** Population size history of Forest & Savanna Elephants

- Applied WF-based inference to real genomic data
- Compared two related species over thousands of generations
:::
::::

---

[← Back to Topics](#toc)
